informal_statement.text: Given a slice of closed-open intervals [start, end), return the maximum number of mutually non-overlapping intervals selectable from the input.
preconditions: for every input interval, start < end
required_properties: the returned count equals the size of some subset of pairwise non-overlapping intervals from the input
no subset of pairwise non-overlapping intervals from the input is strictly larger than the returned count
intervals sharing only an endpoint (interval i ends where interval j starts) are considered non-overlapping
edge_cases: all intervals mutually disjoint (result equals len)
all intervals overlap a common point (result is 1)
intervals touching at a single endpoint

semantic_units.description: Input precondition: every interval satisfies start < stop.
Feasibility: the returned count is witnessed by some subset of the input that is pairwise non-overlapping.
common_failure_modes: confusing index selections with value selections

semantic_units.description: Optimality: no pairwise non-overlapping subset of the input is strictly larger than the returned count.
common_failure_modes: stating only feasibility (admits the trivial return 0)

semantic_units.description: Endpoint convention: two intervals that share only an endpoint are non-overlapping.
common_failure_modes: using strict inequality instead of non-strict at the boundary

semantic_units.description: Empty input: the result is 0 when the input is empty.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (ivs : Intervals), (∀ i ∈ ivs, i.start < i.stop) → ∃ (sel : List Nat), Feasible ivs sel ∧ sel.length = intervalScheduling ivs
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When every input interval has strictly positive length, there exists a feasible selection whose size equals the returned count.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (ivs : Intervals) (sel : List Nat), (∀ i ∈ ivs, i.start < i.stop) → Feasible ivs sel → sel.length ≤ intervalScheduling ivs
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When every input interval has strictly positive length, no feasible selection is strictly larger than the returned count.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (a b : Interval), a.stop = b.start → NonOverlap a b
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: Intervals touching at a single endpoint are non-overlapping.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: theorem spec_max_nonoverlap (intervals : List (Nat × Nat)) (start end : Nat) : True
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: State the overall problem: from a list of closed-open intervals [s,e) represented as (s,e), compute the maximum size of a subset of pairwise non-overlapping intervals.
Define what it means for two intervals to be mutually non-overlapping (closed-open semantics) and what it means for a set/list of intervals to be pairwise non-overlapping.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: def feasible (xs : List Interval) (input : List Interval) : Prop := xs.Nodup ∧ xs ⊆ input ∧ compatible_set xs
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Define feasibility of a chosen subset: it is a subset of the input, has no duplicates, and is pairwise non-overlapping.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: def opt_value (input : List Interval) : Nat := Nat.findGreatest (fun k => ∃ xs, feasible xs input ∧ xs.length = k) (by decide)
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Define the optimal value as the maximum cardinality among all feasible subsets of the input.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: theorem impl_returns_value (input : List Interval) : ∃ k, k = {{rust_reference_result}} ∧ k = opt_value input
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Connect the Rust reference implementation’s returned value to the abstract optimal value (i.e., the implementation computes the optimum).

generated_obligations.lean_statement: theorem impl_produces_feasible_subset (input : List Interval) : ∃ xs, feasible xs input ∧ xs.length = {{rust_reference_result}}
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Show that the algorithm’s constructed/implicit selection corresponds to some feasible subset whose size equals the returned maximum.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: theorem impl_is_upper_bound (input : List Interval) : ∀ xs, feasible xs input → xs.length ≤ {{rust_reference_result}}
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Prove optimality: no feasible subset can be larger than the algorithm’s returned value.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: theorem impl_is_lower_bound (input : List Interval) : ∃ xs, feasible xs input ∧ xs.length = {{rust_reference_result}}
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Prove attainability: there exists a feasible subset of exactly the size returned by the algorithm.
Capture the key greedy-choice property used by the classical interval scheduling algorithm (typically: choosing an interval with earliest finishing time can be extended to an optimal solution).
Provide an exchange argument: any optimal solution can be transformed to include the greedy-selected interval without decreasing the number of intervals.
If the implementation sorts intervals by end time, prove that the sorting preserves the multiset of intervals and that the greedy step uses the correct order.
Prove that the intervals selected by the algorithm are pairwise non-overlapping under closed-open semantics.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: theorem closed_open_semantics (s1 e1 s2 e2 : Nat) : e1 = s2 → ¬ (¬ nonoverlap (s1,e1) (s2,e2))
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Ensure correctness of overlap definition for closed-open intervals: touching at a point (end = start) is allowed (non-overlapping).
Show the Rust reference implementation terminates (e.g., by structural recursion over the input list).

generated_obligations.lean_statement: theorem impl_total (input : List Interval) : ∃ k, k = {{rust_reference_result}}
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Show the implementation is total on all inputs (returns a Nat value for any input list).
